narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dreams despite the challenges many face in this field, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admission system uses a predictive model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, looking at factors like grades, test scores, family background, and study plans to estimate the chances of passing on the first try. For you specifically, the model calculated a 64% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for denial—meaning it flagged your profile as higher risk compared to applicants predicted to pass. The biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 school grade decile, which placed you in the bottom group relative to peers (far below the typical mid-range standing), followed closely by your LSAT score of 19, which is notably lower than the average around 37 that the model associates with stronger outcomes. Your Year 1 grade decile, also in the lowest group versus the usual middle, added further weight toward this concerning prediction. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.40, while below the dataset average of 3.21, actually nudged the model slightly toward expecting success, as did your middle family income bracket and your choice to attend part-time, which is less common (only about 7% of students) but seems linked to better bar passage in the data. Overall, your academic rankings and LSAT stood out as the most influential drags compared to typical students, who often enter with higher deciles around 5-6 and LSATs near 37, tipping the scales against you despite those helpful counterbalances. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just how your numbers stacked up statistically. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted study or additional courses, and perhaps exploring part-time programs that align with your plans while building a stronger academic record. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these key areas and reapplying—your resilience as a Black woman in law will serve you well. Keep pushing; you've got this.
